name: Test Driven Development
scope: A person with BEGINNER proficiency level in Test Driven Development as a competency should be able to explain TDD’s purpose (guiding design, ensuring correctness), describe the RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle, and differentiate TDD from test-after approaches. They can write and structure basic unit tests using frameworks like JUnit, NUnit, xUnit, pytest, Jest, Mocha, unittest, or RSpec, following AAA (Arrange-Act-Assert), naming conventions, and ensuring tests are deterministic, isolated, and automated. They understand the importance of test quality, independence, and clear failure messages, and can maintain simple test code with minimal duplication. They can translate requirements or examples into simple tests, identify happy-path and edge cases, and focus on observable behavior. They know how to isolate code from external dependencies using manual stubs or simple fakes, and are aware of test doubles and mocking tools. They can run tests locally via CLI or IDE, organize test files, and add frameworks to projects using standard build tools. They understand TDD’s role in workflow, commit small test-backed increments, and accept feedback on their tests. They recognize that tests serve as living documentation, maintain test names for clarity, and keep tests up-to-date with changing requirements. They are aware that tests must run reliably in CI pipelines, ensure their tests are included in standard team commands, and understand that fast, reliable unit tests are essential for CI. They know that pairing with experienced practitioners and continuous learning are important for improving TDD skills.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core TDD Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understands the basic purpose of Test Driven Development (TDD): guiding code design and ensuring correctness by writing tests before production code.
- Can explain the simple value proposition of TDD: faster feedback, prevention of regressions, support for refactoring, and improved confidence in changes on small codebases.
- Knows the three key stages of the TDD loop at a conceptual level: RED (failing test), GREEN (make it pass), REFACTOR (improve design with all tests passing).
- Recognizes that TDD is primarily about design and incremental development, not just about having tests.
- Understands the difference between TDD and “test-after” unit testing (e.g., when tests are written and how that affects design and feedback).
- Can distinguish unit tests from higher-level tests (integration, end-to-end) at a conceptual level, and knows that TDD predominantly focuses on unit-level behavior.
- Understands that TDD cycles should be short and frequent, focusing on small increments of behavior.
- Recognizes that TDD focuses on externally visible behavior of small units (functions, methods, classes) rather than internal implementation details.
- Is aware that TDD supports safer refactoring as the test suite acts as a safety net for existing behavior.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Unit Testing Basics & Test Structure","scope_included":"- Understands what a unit test is: an automated, repeatable check of a small piece of code in isolation.
- Knows the common Arrange–Act–Assert (AAA) test structure and can follow it in simple tests.
- Understands basic test naming conventions that describe behavior or scenario and expected outcome (e.g., methodName_condition_expectedResult).
- Recognizes the properties of a good basic unit test: deterministic, isolated, fast, repeatable, and readable.
- Can describe the notion of a single responsibility per test (test focuses on one behavior or closely related behaviors).
- Understands that tests should not depend on external systems (network, file system, databases) for simple unit-level TDD.
- Knows that each test must contain at least one assertion and that assertions compare actual vs. expected results.
- Understands the difference between setup, execution, and verification parts of a test and can place code appropriately.
- Recognizes that tests should be executable automatically (via a command or IDE action), not manually inspected.
- Is aware that test code quality matters (readability, duplication, clarity), even at beginner level.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Executing the TDD Red–Green–Refactor Cycle","scope_included":"- Can perform a simple TDD cycle on a small, self-contained problem (e.g., pure functions, basic class methods).
- RED: Can write a small test first that fails for the intended reason (e.g., missing implementation, wrong result).
- GREEN: Can write the minimal amount of production code needed to make the new failing test pass, without over-engineering or adding unrelated behavior.
- REFACTOR: Understands that refactoring means changing internal structure without changing observable behavior and that tests must stay green during refactoring.
- Can run the full test suite frequently (after each small change or a few changes) to check for regressions.
- Understands that TDD encourages working in tiny, incremental steps rather than large design jumps.
- Recognizes and can avoid common beginner mistakes such as writing too much production code before running tests or skipping the refactor step entirely.
- Can gradually extend behavior by adding new tests for additional cases (e.g., edge cases, error paths) and repeating the TDD cycle.
- Can stop and identify which part of the cycle they are in and what the next appropriate action is (write test vs. write code vs. refactor).","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Basic TDD-Oriented Design Awareness","scope_included":"- Understands that designing code to be testable is a goal of TDD and that testability often improves overall design.
- Recognizes that smaller, focused functions and classes are easier to drive with tests and to reason about.
- Has basic awareness that tight coupling and heavy dependencies make TDD harder, even if they cannot yet refactor complex cases.
- Understands that TDD encourages defining behavior and public interfaces before internal implementation details.
- Recognizes the idea that tests act as a living specification of the behavior of small units.
- Is aware that TDD can help avoid over-design by implementing only what is currently required by tests.
- Understands that clear input/output contracts (parameters, return values) are easier to drive through unit tests than hidden global state.
- Can make simple design choices that support TDD, such as preferring pure functions or simple collaborators when possible at beginner level.
- Understands at a basic level that refactoring supported by tests is a continuous activity, not a one-time step at the end of a project.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Working with Requirements, Examples, and Behavior","scope_included":"- Understands that tests in TDD represent concrete examples of desired behavior derived from requirements or user stories.
- Can translate a simple requirement into one or more example-based tests (e.g., “given X input, expect Y output”).
- Recognizes that starting from examples helps clarify ambiguous or incomplete requirements for small features.
- Can identify basic happy-path and a few edge-case scenarios to cover with tests (e.g., normal input vs. empty input, invalid input).
- Is aware that TDD focuses on observable behavior and business rules rather than only on internal algorithm details.
- Understands that failing tests serve as a signal that desired behavior is not yet met and that they should not be ignored or commented out.
- Recognizes the benefit of using descriptive test names to capture intent and business meaning, not just mechanics.
- Is able to adjust or extend tests when requirements change in a straightforward, small-scope scenario.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Quality and Maintainability of Tests (Beginner Level)","scope_included":"- Understands that test code is production-quality code and must be maintained over time.
- Recognizes that overly complex tests are a smell and may indicate poor design or overuse of implementation details in tests.
- Knows that each test should be independent from other tests, not relying on their execution order or side effects.
- Understands that flakiness (tests that sometimes pass, sometimes fail) is undesirable and often caused by external dependencies or shared mutable state.
- Can keep test data and setup simple and explicit for small, beginner-level problems.
- Is able to remove obvious duplication between tests using basic refactoring techniques (e.g., helper methods, simple setup), while staying within their comfort zone.
- Understands that tests should fail with clear, understandable messages so that the cause of failures is easier to locate.
- Is aware that a growing set of tests gradually builds confidence and acts as a regression safety net, even if the suite is still small.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Handling Simple Dependencies and Isolation","scope_included":"- Understands at a conceptual level why isolating the unit under test from external systems (database, network, file system) is useful for TDD.
- Can write basic unit tests that do not require real external resources by using simple in-memory data structures or stubs they create manually.
- Is aware of the concept of test doubles (stub, fake, mock) even if they cannot yet apply advanced mocking techniques.
- Recognizes that tests that depend on external state or remote services are slow and brittle and are not ideal for TDD cycles.
- In simple scenarios, can replace external calls with simplified collaborators or predefined inputs to keep tests deterministic.
- Understands that not every test requires sophisticated mocking tools at beginner level; simple handcrafted replacements are often enough for initial practice.
- Is aware that more complex isolation techniques and mocking frameworks exist and will be needed at higher proficiency levels.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Basic Tooling and Environment for TDD","scope_included":"- Can identify and use a unit testing framework in at least one language (e.g., JUnit, NUnit, xUnit, pytest, Jest, Mocha, unittest, RSpec) at a basic level.
- Knows how to create a new test file or test class and add simple test functions/methods recognized by the testing framework.
- Can run tests locally using the command line, IDE, or editor integration and interpret basic success/failure output.
- Understands the difference between running a single test, a group of tests, and the entire test suite.
- Can use basic assertions provided by the testing framework (e.g., equality, inequality, truthiness, null/undefined checks, exceptions).
- Knows how to organize tests in a simple directory or module structure (e.g., tests alongside code or in a dedicated tests folder) consistent with common practices in their environment.
- Is aware of basic configuration concepts such as test discovery patterns or naming conventions required by the framework.
- Is able to add the testing framework and related dependencies to a project using the standard build or dependency management tools of their environment at a beginner level.","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Workflow, Collaboration, and Feedback (Beginner TDD Practice)","scope_included":"- Can describe where TDD fits within a basic development workflow: pick a small task, write a failing test, implement, refactor, repeat.
- Understands that TDD is performed continuously during development, not just at the end of a feature or sprint.
- Can explain their TDD steps and reasoning to peers in simple terms (what they chose to test, why they wrote a particular test first).
- Is able to work in small increments that are suitable for TDD within a shared codebase (e.g., small methods or small behavioral additions).
- Understands that version control commits should ideally represent small, test-backed steps.
- Is receptive to feedback from code reviews about tests (naming, coverage of cases, clarity) and can make simple improvements based on that feedback.
- Recognizes that TDD requires discipline to resist writing large chunks of code without tests and is willing to practice that discipline on basic tasks.
- Understands that pairing or mobbing with more experienced TDD practitioners is beneficial to learn patterns and habits, even if they cannot yet lead such sessions.","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Documentation, Naming, and Traceability via Tests","scope_included":"- Understands that well-named tests document expected behavior for future readers, including their future self.
- Can write test names that clearly express intent, inputs, and expected outcomes for simple behaviors.
- Recognizes that tests can serve as executable examples of how to use functions or classes and can be referred to by others.
- Is able to add minimal comments to tests where necessary to clarify non-obvious intent, without over-commenting straightforward behavior.
- Understands the importance of keeping tests up to date when behavior or requirements change, to avoid tests becoming misleading documentation.
- Is aware of basic ways to relate tests to requirements or user stories (e.g., including ticket IDs in test names or comments) in simple team workflows.
- Recognizes that overly generic or cryptic test names reduce the documentation value and tries to avoid them.","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Continuous Integration Awareness and Running Tests in Pipelines (Intro Level)","scope_included":"- Understands at a high level that tests written for TDD should also run in automated pipelines (CI) to catch regressions.
- Knows that tests must be fast and reliable to be effective in CI, reinforcing the importance of unit-level tests.
- Is aware that failing tests in CI indicate issues that need to be addressed before changes are considered safe to merge.
- Can ensure that new tests they write locally are included in the standard test command used by the team or CI system.
- Recognizes that adding or modifying tests without breaking existing tests is a basic expectation before pushing code.
- Is aware that more advanced CI configurations (parallelization, test reporting, coverage) exist and will become relevant at higher proficiency levels, but understands that their current responsibility is to ensure their tests run and pass consistently.","is_non_core_section":true}]}
